# Transcrição: 01-Speaking with Confidence

- **Fonte:** 01-Speaking with Confidence
- **Duração:** 09:43
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

I had to jump in here real quick and just say before you get started, remember anything in life that has a worthy outcome, take some effort and energy, you want to have a better body, you've got to go to the gym. You want to take your income to another level, you've got to transform what you're doing, take the knowledge you have and transmute it in a different direction. None of it is easy when we start. It's worth it. Let me give you a little secret. Focus on the outcome. I don't like going to the gym. I've just got to be honest with you, but I go every day. Why? Because I love the way I feel when I'm done. I love that it's influenced my children and my two older ones work out. I love looking good for my wife. I love feeling healthier, so when I focus on the outcome, I can't wait to do it. What's the outcome you can focus on right now of finishing this course and taking the action and sticking with it so you can live into your full potential, so you can impact others, so you can have no glass ceiling and truly live into your full potential. Stick with it as you move forward. Do the work. I'll see you at the end. The ability to speak confidently and clearly is critical to your success in both your professional and personal lives. I'm excited to share with you ways that we can hone our messages. Maintain our communication and deliver it in a compelling and informative way. Before you can ever start your communication, you need to know what your clear goal and purpose are. To me, a goal has three parts. Information, emotion, and action. What do we want our audience to know about the topic we're presenting? How do we want them to feel and what do we want them to do? We have to hone and craft each of these components to make sure our message resonates with our audience, which means we have to think about our audience. We have to do some reconnaissance, reflection, and research to best understand how we can tailor what it is we're saying to their needs, which means we need to think about their knowledge level on our topic. Do they know a lot or do they know a little? What kind of scaffolding do we need to do to help them best learn from us? We also have to think about their attitudes. Are they likely to be in favor of what we're saying, curious, or might they be a little agnostic or unsure? Again, we'll have to adjust our message to make sure it targets their attitudes. Perhaps most important, we need to think about what are the areas of resistance or concern and hesitation that they might have. Is it that it takes too much time, costs too much? Think about the specific resistance points so we can address those as we craft our messages. By taking time to think about our audience and their needs, we can best focus what it

## [03:01 - 06:01]

is we're trying to communicate. It helps us prioritize what we want to say, and it helps our audience to better get our message, to learn it and to act on it. Thinking about your audience is critical, and we do that to inform our goal. We also have to think about our abilities and our strengths. What makes us a good communicator? I'd like each of you to think about the things that work for you. Perhaps it's telling stories, or maybe it's leveraging different bits of information and knitting them together so your audience can understand. By taking the time to think about your strengths, it can really help. We also have to think about the complexity of the messages that we're sharing. The goal is not to dumb things down or oversimplify. We actually have to take the time to make our content accessible. Again, knowing a lot about our audience will help. So you might think about what are some of the stories you can tell to help establish the information you want to communicate to help make it relatable. Think about analogies and comparisons you could use to help your audience connect what they know to what they don't know. Think also about ways of deconstructing your content. Could you graph it out in some kind of chart or process flow diagram? By taking the time to think about the complexities of your material and how to make them accessible, you can really help your audience. So we've thought about our goal, we've thought about our audience, and we've thought about some of our strengths when it comes to communication. Now we have to think about the environment in which we speak. Many of us are communicating virtually. That means we have to think about how we frame ourselves. What's behind us? Is it consistent with what we're saying? Does it help our audience focus or might it distract? How do we frame ourselves in the actual screen? Do we fill half the screen with our bodies so they can see us completely? Or do we want to have some kind of visual that's sharing the screen with us? We need to think about these things because how you come off is not just what you say, it's how you say it. So let's think about how we can use our body and our voice to help our audience see us as confident and compelling. The first thing we have to do is think about our body posture. Regardless of if you're in person or virtual, how you hold your body matters. I'd like you to think of three things, being big, balanced, and still. To make ourselves big, take your shoulder blades and just pull them down. This broadens your body. You look big. Nervous people, uncertain people, make themselves small. So pull those shoulder blades back. You want your head to be straight, not tilted. Again, we don't want to be rigid, but simply pulling the shoulder blades down and holding

## [06:01 - 09:05]

our head straight help us look confident. And certainly we want to remain still. If we're seated, many people will slide side to side in their chair. Sometimes people when they're standing will sway. Being big, balanced, and still conveys confidence. We also have to think about what we do with our hands. Gesturing is really important. And when we gesture, just one simple rule. Go beyond your shoulders. Many of us when we gesture, we gesture in tight. We look like a dinosaur, nervous. Simply go broader than your shoulders and you look open and expressive. We have to make sure that we make good eye contact as well. Eye contact is how we connect. If you're speaking virtually, you need to make sure that you look at the camera. The camera is your portal to the audience that you're speaking to. If I'm in person, I need to make sure that I look at everybody in the room. Not in a patterned way, but look at some in the back, some up front. We also have to vary our voice. Your voice is a wind instrument. The more breath you have, the more you can do with your voice. I can raise it, I can lower it, I can go faster or slower. The use of your voice keeps your audience engaged. Our brains are wired for novelty. If something were to stay the same, we lose interest and pay less attention to it. So we want to be big balanced and still. We want to gesture beyond our shoulders, make good eye contact, and we want to vary our voice. So by combining our thoughts on our goal and message, as well as thinking about our audience so we can really hone the message to them and make it accessible, we can become much more effective communicators. So how do we practice this? The only way you get better at communication is three things. Repetition, reflection, and feedback. You have to get the reps in. Think of yourself as an athlete. You have to do it over and over again. Do drills to prepare. A great way to do this is to record yourself. It's incredibly painful to watch yourself on video, but it makes a big difference. When I teach my graduate students, they record all of their presentations, and they have to watch it not once, not twice, but three times. By watching it, they get to see what they do in their physical presence. I then make them listen to it without watching, and they hear what their voice does. And then finally, they watch it and listen to it together, and they're able to see completely different things to work on. The goal of repetition is to help you feel more comfortable. The goal of reflection is to help you change. When we watch ourselves, we tend to only focus on the negative. I encourage all of you to look at the things you're doing well. The goal is to enhance the strengths and change the things we need to strengthen.

## [09:05 - 09:43]

Finally, we need to get feedback. We are not the best judges of our own communication. We need to solicit ideas from mentors, friends, and colleagues who can help us see how they're interpreting what it is we're saying. Repetition, reflection, and feedback. Recording yourself, getting input. This is how you improve your communication. Taken together, focusing on your audience and your goal, thinking about your message and making it accessible, thinking about your environment and your nonverbal presence. These are the skills that will help you improve your communication.
